coredata.dc:title: A reversible photoluminescence based on cerium-doped carbon dots for “on–off-on” dual detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate ions with live cell application

coredata.dc:description: In this study, the development of cerium- and nitrogen doped carbon dots (Ce-N-CDs) as a highly sensitive and reversible photoluminescent probe for dual detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate (PPi) ions with live-cell applications. The Ce-N-CDs were synthesized via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using ribavirin and cerium nitrate, fabricating yellow-emissive Ce-N-CDs with an average diameter of 3.62 ± 0.2 nm and a quantum yield of 17.2 %. A variety of methods were used to reveal the properties of the yellow-emissive Ce-N-CDs as prepared, including UV–Visible, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. These Ce-N-CDs showed excellent stability in their PL properties under a wide pH range, high ionic strength, UV exposure, and long-term storage. Notably, Fe3+ ions selectively quenched the PL of Ce-N-CDs, while the subsequent addition of PPi restored the PL signal establishing a reliable “on–off–on” detection mechanism. The sensor displayed impressive sensitivity, with detection limits of 34.6 nM for Fe3+ and 25.4 nM for PPi, along with excellent linear correlations (R2 = 0.9963 and 0.9951, respectively). Furthermore, the Ce-N-CDs sensor consistently maintained its performance across six consecutive detection cycles of Fe3+ and PPi, demonstrating outstanding reversibility and recyclability. Furthermore, cytotoxicity assays confirmed the high biocompatibility of Ce-N-CDs in HCT-116 cells, and confocal imaging validated their capability for intracellular ion monitoring. Overall, this work introduces a novel, low-toxicity, and recyclable dual-sensing nano-platform with significant potential in biomedical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and live-cell applications.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Vellore Institute of Technology deserves our sincere appreciation for their invaluable contributions to our research, particularly in providing laboratory and instrumental support. We also extend our gratitude to DST-VIT-FIST for their support. The authors would also like to thank Ongoing Research Funding Program (ORF-2025-668), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF) at University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011\u20132015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017\u20132018). Moreover, he worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018\u20132020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

Selectivity measurements of (a) PL spectra, (b) PL intensity profile, and (c) daylight and 365 nm UV light photographs of Ce-N-CDs solution (2 mg/mL) with the addition of various metal ions (10 μM) in PBS (pH ∼ 7.4). (d) PL spectra of the Ce-N-CDs aqueous solutions (2 mg/mL) upon the addition of different Fe3+ ions concentrations (0–10 μM) in pH ∼ 7.4 PBS solution, (e) Jobs plot analysis of the Ce-N-CDs intensity vs. mole fraction of Fe3+ ion, (f) Stern-Volmer plot of the Ce-N-CDs intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion (0–10 μM), and (g) the calculation of LOD based on the Ce-N-CDs intensity in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion (0–10 μM).

Interference selectivity measurements of (a) PL spectra and (b) PL intensity profile Ce-N-CDs (2 mg/mL) and Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ solution with the addition of various anions and biomolecules (50 μM) in PBS (pH ∼ 7.4). (c) PL spectra of the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ aqueous solutions upon the addition of different PPi ion concentrations (0–50 μM) in pH ∼ 7.4 PBS solution, (d) Jobs plot analysis of the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ intensity vs. mole fraction of PPi ion, (e) Stern-Volmer plot of the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ intensity in the absence and presence of PPi ion (0–50 μM), and (f) the calculation of LOD based on the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ intensity in the absence and presence of PPi ion (0–50 μM).
(a) Recovery measurements and (b) The reversible switching cycles of the PL spectra and their intensity of Ce-N-CDs, Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ and in the presence of PPi solution in PBS (pH ∼ 7.4).

Cerium- and nitrogen doped carbon dots (Ce-N-CDs) and their dual detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate (PPi) ions.

head.ce:title: A reversible photoluminescence based on cerium-doped carbon dots for “on–off-on” dual detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate ions with live cell application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the development of cerium- and nitrogen doped carbon dots (Ce-N-CDs) as a highly sensitive and reversible photoluminescent probe for dual detection of Fe3+ and pyrophosphate (PPi) ions with live-cell applications. The Ce-N-CDs were synthesized via a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using ribavirin and cerium nitrate, fabricating yellow-emissive Ce-N-CDs with an average diameter of 3.62 ± 0.2 nm and a quantum yield of 17.2 %. A variety of methods were used to reveal the properties of the yellow-emissive Ce-N-CDs as prepared, including UV–Visible, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. These Ce-N-CDs showed excellent stability in their PL properties under a wide pH range, high ionic strength, UV exposure, and long-term storage. Notably, Fe3+ ions selectively quenched the PL of Ce-N-CDs, while the subsequent addition of PPi restored the PL signal establishing a reliable “on–off–on” detection mechanism. The sensor displayed impressive sensitivity, with detection limits of 34.6 nM for Fe3+ and 25.4 nM for PPi, along with excellent linear correlations (R2 = 0.9963 and 0.9951, respectively). Furthermore, the Ce-N-CDs sensor consistently maintained its performance across six consecutive detection cycles of Fe3+ and PPi, demonstrating outstanding reversibility and recyclability. Furthermore, cytotoxicity assays confirmed the high biocompatibility of Ce-N-CDs in HCT-116 cells, and confocal imaging validated their capability for intracellular ion monitoring. Overall, this work introduces a novel, low-toxicity, and recyclable dual-sensing nano-platform with significant potential in biomedical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and live-cell applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (III) is thepivotalelementin human or livingtissue becauseofitsrolein biological systems. Maintaining the proper concentration of ferric ions is crucial, as both deficiency and excess can lead to serious diseases or disorders [1]. Excess Fe3+ ionsin the cells of livingbeingsis toxic, which can cause vomiting, diarrhea and intestinal injury. Furthermore, Fe3+ is greatly oxidized, whereas compounds like vitamins are reductive [2]. Fe3+ ion detection possesses various merits, particularly in environmental monitoring, biomedical testing, and industrial processes [3]. Fe3+ is an essential metal ion in living organisms, but an excess would induce oxidative stress and pollution in the environment. Various sensing platforms, including fluorescence, colorimetry, and electrochemical sensors, are able to detect Fe3+ selectively and sensitively at trace concentrations [4]. Fluorescence probes such as carbon dots-based probes provide rapid, inexpensive, and non-invasive estimation with high specificity via fluorescence quenching characteristics. The detection of pyrophosphate (PPi) is also important in biomedicine, environmental science, and industry. Pyrophosphate is an essential compound in living systems, specifically in energy metabolism, DNA synthesis, and mineralization of bone [5]. Its reliable detection is used for the diagnosis of diseases such as arthritis, renal failure, and some forms of cancer. Fluorescence, colorimetric, and electrochemical sensors provide sensitive and selective detection techniques for PPi, which make rapid and cost-effective analysis possible [6,7]. PPi is utilized in foods as an additive, toothpaste, detergents, and baking powder [8]. Though PPi is a valuable substance, excessive consumption of PPi adversely affects body health [9]. PPi in excess or lower level in biological system may leads to a number of diseases including chondrocalcinosis, calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease and rheumatologic disease [10]. Nonetheless, fluorogenic chemosensor methods are regarded as being superior to the other analytical procedures for the detection of pyrophosphate (PPi) owing to their remarkable sensitivity, short response time, and affordability [11]. In spite of these limitations, continuing developments in sensor technology are progressively enhancing the sensitivity, specificity, and cost-effectiveness of PPi detection, rendering it increasingly useful in biomedical and environmental fields.

Since their discovery in 2004, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a unique family of zero-dimensional luminescent particles, capturing the interest of researchers in a wide range of fields. Carbon dots are a new generation of ultrasmall materials with sizes between 2–10 nm [12]. The fluorescent carbon dots have their impressive advantages include low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability, good water solubility, versatile surface functionalities, simple modification, and unique optical properties [13–26]. Typically, carbon dots are 0-dimensional nanomaterials with a carbon core having sp2/sp3 hybridization and surface functional groups depending on different core architectures, photoluminescence properties, and carbon precursors, CDs are often classified into four materials based on their fundamental structure and synthesis route: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [27]. The methods currently being used may be broadly categorized as top-down and bottom-up. The top-down methods include decomposing massive carbon materials like graphite, graphene, CNTs, and fullerenes into nanoparticles through processes such as laser ablation, electrochemical treatments, ball milling, arc discharge, and chemical oxidation [28]. Alternatively, the bottom-up method emphasizes the synthesis of carbon dots through a “polymerization-carbonization” process in which tiny molecular precursors are converted using procedures like hydrothermal carbonization, solvothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment, pyrolytic carbonization, and other similar processes [29].

Rare earth metal-doped carbon dots (RE-CDs) are novel nanodots engineered to improve the optical, electronic, and catalytic behavior of the traditional carbon dots (CDs) [30]. The addition of rare-earth metals such as europium (Eu) [31], gadolinium (Gd) [32,33], dysprosium (Dy) [34], terbium (Tb) [35], praseodymium (Pr) [36], and cerium (Ce) [37,38] to the carbon dot matrix considerably enhances PLintensity, fluorescence lifetime, and quantum yield. In recent years, CDs have been extensively explored for ion sensing due to their excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and high water solubility. Among these, rare earth-doped CDs, particularly cerium-doped variants, offer superior optical performance, quantum yield, and environmental resilience. Several recent studies have emphasized the need for advanced materials that combine high selectivity, sensitivity, and stability for real-time, reversible ion detection in complex biological environments [39–42]. In biomedical applications, RE-CDs are efficient probes for fluorescence imaging and are capable of being used in the process of photodynamic theory (PDT) application for cancer treatment due to their potential for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) [43–45]. The advantages of transition metal-doped carbon dots (TM-CDs) and rare earth metal-doped carbon dots (RE-CDs) depend on the application, but overall, rare earth metal-doped carbon dots (RE-CDs) possess greater advantages in terms of optical performance, stability, and biomedical use. RE-CDs show stronger photoluminescence, extended fluorescence lifetimes, and better quantum yields compared to TM-CDs. They are extremely effective in applications of bioimaging, optical sensing, and anti-counterfeiting methodologies.

In this sense, PL CDs have exhibited great potential in the specific and sensitive detection of ions due to their unique PLcharacteristics. One of the most commonly used ways is PL-based sensing, which uses quenching and recovery mechanisms [46–48]. CDs undergoPL quenching in the presence of ions due to interactions between surface functional groups and metal ions, which mainly occur via electron transfer or inner filter effects [49,50]. The subsequent addition of PPi, a powerful cation chelator, reduces the interaction between cations and CDs, resulting in PL recovery. This reversible “turn-off/on” PL reaction is the basis of very efficient cations and PPi ionsdetection methods [51]. CDshigh selectivity, biocompatibility, and water dispersibility have resulted in broad use in biomedical diagnostics,environmental monitoring,and industrial applications.

In this study, yellow-emitting Ce-N-CDs were produced using ribavirin and cerium nitrate as precursors (Scheme 1 ). The Ce-N-CDs were characterized by UV–Vis, PL spectroscopy, FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS analysisto confirm the optical, surface, morphological, and elemental properties. Ce-N-CDs emitted strong PL at 553 nm (excitation at 397 nm) with an average size of 3.62 ± 0.2 nm. They demonstrated outstanding PL stability throughout pH ranges, as well as high ionic strength and UV exposure over an extended period of time. Furthermore, Fe3+ selectively reduced the PL intensity and PPi restored it, allowing for sensitive and specific detection. Additionally, the quantitative analysis showed strong linearity for Fe3+ (R2 = 0.9963, LOD = 34.6 nM) and PPi (R2 = 0.9951, LOD = 25.4 nM). The sensor demonstrated high reversibility and recyclability over six cycles and was effective in detecting Fe3+ and PPi. Finally, the biocompatibility tests confirmed the non-toxic nature of Ce-N-CDs, supporting their potential in live-cell imaging and biomedical applications.

Ribavirin and cerium nitrate hexahydrate [Ce(NO3)3·6H2O] were obtained from TCI Chemical Company, South Korea. Metal nitrates and chloride salts were obtained from Merck. Ultra-pure water was supplied using a Milli-Q purification system. All other solvents and reagents used were of analytical grade and employed without further purification.

Ce-N-CDs were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal approach with modified from a previously reported method. In a typical procedure, 0.10 g of ribavirin (serving as both a carbon and nitrogen source) and 0.15 g of cerium nitrate Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were accurately weighed and dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water under continuous stirring. The resultant mixture was exposed to ultrasonic treatment (sonication) for 10 min to ensure complete dissolution and homogeneous dispersion of the precursors. Following sonication, the clear solution was transferred into an 80 mL autoclave, which was then sealed and placed in a preheated oven at 180 °C for 8 h to undergo hydrothermal carbonization. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to RT. The obtained reaction combination was then centrifuged (or allowed to settle) to remove any large or insoluble particulates. The supernatant was sensibly filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane filter to further eliminate any remaining aggregates or precipitates. Finally, the purified aqueous solution containing Ce-N-CDs was subjected to freeze-drying (lyophilization) to obtain the final product in powder form, which was stored in a desiccator for further use and characterization.

To investigate the selectivity of Ce-N-CDs toward various cations, stock solutions of different potentially interfering metal ions were prepared at a concentration of 10 μM in distilled water. These included Co2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Sr2+. For PL measurements, a standard detection system was prepared by mixing 1.975 mL of solvent (distilled water) with 10 μL of the selected metal ion solution. Subsequently, 25 μL of Ce-N-CDs (2 mg/mL) was added to the mixture in a 3 mL quartz cuvette. The resulting mixture was vortexed to ensure homogeneity, and after a standing time of 5 min at room temperature, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a spectrofluorometer.

To assess the anti-interference capability of the Ce-N-CDs system, a separate set of experiments was conducted in which 10 μM Fe3+ solutions were combined with various common anions and biologically relevant molecules (each at 50 μM). These included: acetate (Ac−), thiocyanate (SCN−), carbonate (CO3 2−), bromide (Br−), fluoride (F−), chloride (Cl−), bicarbonate (HCO3 −), sulfate (SO4 2−), hydrogen sulfate (HSO4 −), nitrite (NO2 −), sulfide (S2−), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), pyrophosphate (P2O7 4− PPi), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), and adenosine monophosphate (AMP). To further investigate the sensitivity of the Ce-N-CDs for PPi detection in the occurrence of Fe3+, mixtures containing 50 μM PPi and 2.0 mg/mL Ce-N-CDs were prepared.

To assess the cytotoxicity and cellular utilization of Ce-N-CDs in HCT-116 cancer cells, the WST assay was performed. HCT-116 cells were seeded onto 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well. They were incubated overnight at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. After the incubation period each well contained 100 µL of DMEM treated with penicillin (100 µL/mL), streptomycin (100 µL/mL), and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), coupled with Ce-N-CDs at a concentration of 75 µg/mL. The treated cells were then incubated for another 24 h before being rinsed three times with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove any remaining materials. Next, add a 5 µg/mL WST solution to each well and incubation for 4 h. To dissolve the formazan crystals generated during the experiment, 100 µL of DMSO was added after medium evaporation and the cell viability was measured using the following formula:
% I n h i b i t i o n = 100 -
O D _ s a m p l e / O D _ c o n t r o l
× 100
Additionally, the cellular imaging potential of Ce-N-CDs, Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+, and Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ + PPi was assessed in HCT-116 cells through CLSM using an IX-81 Olympus microscope (Japan), with images captured at excitation/emission wavelengths of 400 nm/550 nm.

The optical behavior of Ce-N-CDs was characterized by UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The UV–Vis spectrum showed an absorption peak near 295 nm, which corresponds to π–π* transitions (Fig. 1 a). These transitions occur when electrons in the conjugated π-bonding orbitals of sp2-hybridized carbon domains absorb energy and are excited to higher-energy π* antibonding orbitals [52]. This transition reflects the graphitic nature of the carbon core and is a key indicator of the conjugated electronic structure that contributes to the strong PL of the Ce-N-CDs. In addition, a broad absorption around 403 nm was observed, which is attributed to n–π* transitions. These transitions involve the excitation of non-bonding (n) electrons typically from lone pairs on heteroatoms like oxygen or nitrogen into π* antibonding orbitals. The presence of n–π* transitions indicates surface defect states introduced by nitrogen and cerium doping, which are crucial for tuning the electronic structure and enhancing PL properties. The Ce-N-CDs showed good dispersion, with the solution being pale yellow under daylight. Upon UV illumination, they gave a distinct bright yellow luminescence (inset of Fig. 1a). TherewasasharpPL emission peakat 553 nm and maximum excited at the optimal wavelength of 397 nm (Fig. 1b) with a quantum yield (QY) of 17.2 % was calculated using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (quantum yield = 54 %) as the reference standard under identical experimental conditions. The emission spectra of Ce-N-CDs at different excitation wavelength (Fig. 1c) [53]. There was a sharp PL peak at 553 nm upon excitation at 397 nm. The redshift indicated by the change in the normalized PL from 350 nm to 470 nm reflects the gradual shift of the longer wavelengths (Fig. 1d) [53]. Notably, this normalization of the emission spectra by intensity enables clearer visualization of the shift in emission peak wavelength at different excitation energies, facilitating interpretation of excitation-dependent PL behavior. Interestingly, the normalized PL spectra not only exhibit an excitation-dependent redshift in emission maxima but also reveal changes in the relative intensity of emission bands. This observation suggests that the emission may not originate from a single, uniform electronic system but rather from multiple emissive centers, possibly associated with surface defects, dopant-induced states, or heterogeneously distributed fluorophores. This behavior aligns with previously reported studies on carbon dot systems exhibiting multicolor or excitation-dependent PL due to structurally distinct emissive sites [54].

As illustrated in Fig. S1a, Ce-N-CDs display a strong pH-dependent photoluminescence (PL) response across a broad pH range (3–13). From pH 3 to 7, PL intensity gradually increases, which may result from protonation effects or enhanced structural stability under acidic conditions. In contrast, above pH 8, PL intensity steadily decreases, likely due to deprotonation or structural degradation in alkaline environments [55]. This distinct trend confirms the high sensitivity of Ce-N-CDs to pH fluctuations, highlighting their potential for pH-responsive fluorescence sensing. Fig. S1b investigates the effect of ionic capacity on PL performance by changing the concentration of sodium chloride from 0 to 1.0 M. At minimal concentrations, the level of PL intensityis relatively steady, suggesting negligible influence from ionic interactions [56]. These findings indicate that Ce-N-CDs can serve as reliable optical sensors to monitor variations in ionic strength in aqueous environments. Furthermore, the Ce-N-CDsstability under extended illumination, PL intensitywas measured over 24 h of continuous light exposure (Fig. S1c). The intensity was consistent throughout, indicating exceptional photostability and resistance to photodegradation [57]. This stability under sustained excitation indicates that Ce-N-CDs retain their structural and optical integrity, making them well-suited for long-term optoelectronic and fluorescence-based applications. Additionally, the long-term storage stability was further evaluated over a 90-day period (Fig. S1d). Initially, PL intensity is at its peak, reflecting intact structural and emission properties. A slow, steady decrease is observed over time, which may result from minor structural changes or environmental influences such as moisture and oxygen exposure. However, the overall loss is minimal, indicating that Ce-N-CDs maintain a high level of PL performance over extended periods. This further supports their suitability for applications requiring prolonged functionality, including bioimaging, sensing, and other optical technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological properties of Ce-N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a illustrates the FTIR spectroscopy results, which were used to identify the functional groups adsorbed on the surface of Ce-N-CDs. The absorption bands between 3200 and 3500 cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of–OH and N–H [58]. The peaks seen between 2932 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1 are due to stretching vibration ofC–H. The prominent absorption peaks at 1659 cm−1 correspond to the –C=O group. Furthermore,the1417 and 1355 cm−1 absorption peakscorrespond to stretching vibrations ofC=C and C-N. The bands between 1033 cm−1 show a C-O group [59]. A band at 475 cm−1 is linked to the Ce-O bond stretching vibrations, which confirms cerium-oxygen interactions. These observations indicate that hydrophilic groups are extensively present on the surface of Ce-N-CDs, consistent with the FTIR spectrum. The XRD pattern of Ce-N-CDs revealed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ ≈ 21.7°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating a predominantly amorphous or disordered graphitic structure (Fig. 2b) [59]. Furthermore, the weak shoulder peak at ∼ 40.4° is observed, which is attributed to disordered carbon with a high degree of atoms crystallinity, corresponding to the (100) plane of a hexagonal graphite structure. This amorphous nature is advantageous for sensing applications, as it typically implies a higher density of surface defects and functional groups, which can serve as active sites for interaction with target ions. These structural irregularities facilitate efficient surface adsorption and stronger interaction with metal ions, leading to more pronounced changes in PL intensity. Therefore, the XRD results support the suitability of Ce-N-CDs as a responsive and effective platform for ions detection and live-cell imaging. Additionally, the TEM and HRTEM was performed to analyze the size, morphology, and nanoscale features of Ce-N-CDs. TEM images reveal that the Ce-N-CDs are almost spherical and well-dispersed in water without visible aggregation [60] (Fig. 2c). The observed lattice fringes show a d-spacing of 0.21 nm, resultant to characteristic crystal planes of cerium-containing compounds. This well-ordered lattice structure reflects a high degree of crystallinity and suggests strong interactions between cerium and the N-CDs [60]. The particle size distribution in Fig. 2d was obtained by analyzing over 50 particles from the TEM images (Fig. 2c) using imageJ software. While the average diameter was found to be approximately 3.62 ± 0.2 nm size range from 2.0 to 5.5 nm, the presence of some polydispersity is evident. This is typical of hydrothermally synthesized CDs. The XRD analysis (Fig. 2b), which reflects only the crystalline domain, showed a broad peak corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting an amorphous-to-semi-crystalline structure. This accounts for the difference in apparent sizes between XRD and TEM data.

The XPS analysis confirmed the successful doping of Ce, N, C, and O elements into the Ce-N-CDs structure [53]. The full XPS spectrum of Ce-N-CDs displays four distinct peaks at binding energies of 904.3 eV, 400.1 eV, 286.4 eV, and 533.8 eV, conforming to Ce3d, N1s, C1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The high-resolution Ce3d spectra showed two different peaks 885.6 eV and 902.3 eV, corresponding to Ce3+ and Ce4+ oxidation states (Fig. 3b), which resemble to Ce3d5/2 and Ce3d3/2, respectively [55]. The coexistence of these mixed valence states is crucial, as Ce3+ can act as an electron donor while Ce4+ serves as an electron acceptor, enabling efficient redox cycling and enhanced charge transfer interactions with target ions.

Additionally, the N1s spectrum further supports nitrogen doping and reveals four peaks at 397.5 eV, 398.7 eV, 399.6 eV, and 406.3 eV, given to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, amide-N, and graphitic-N, respectively [52] (Fig. 3c). These N configurations contribute to better dispersion in aqueous media and stronger binding affinity toward target ions, further facilitating the “turn-off” response. Moreover, the C1s spectrum shows three different peaks at 281.5 eV, 283.1 eV, and 284.6 eV, corresponding to sp2/sp3 hybridized C–C/C=C, C–N, and C–O–C/C–OH bonds [60] (Fig. 3d). As illustrated in Fig. 3e, the O1s spectrum displays three peaks at 528.8 eV, 531.0 eV, and 532.1 eV, which are attributed to metal oxides, C=O, and C–O–C/O–H groups, respectively. These oxygen-containing functional groups play a significant role in enhancing the stability and reactivity of Ce-N-CDs. Based on these, the XPS analysis verifies the FTIR findings and confirms that Ce and N were effectively incorporated into the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity experiments involving various metal ions were conducted the sensing ability of Ce-N-CDs. To explore their potential application as a photoluminescence (PL) probe, the effect of different cations on the PL intensity of Ce-N-CDs was investigated. To assess the sensing properties of Ce-N-CDs, selectivity tests were conducted in a PBS (pH 7.4) solution. PL intensity of Ce-N-CDs in the presence of cations, such as Co2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, and Sr2+ (10 μM) has been illustrated in Fig. 4 a and 4b. Among all tested metal ions, Fe3+ exhibited the most significant quenching effect on the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of Ce-N-CDs [52], particularly at the emission wavelength of 553 nm. This strong quenching suggests a specific interaction between Fe3+ and Ce-N-CDs, likely due to electron transfer or complex formation [52]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Ce-N-CDs displayed a bright yellow emission under 365 nm UV illumination, which gradually faded to colorless upon the addition of Fe3+ ions. In contrast, other metal ions at similar concentrations did not induce any noticeable change in PL color or intensity, highlighting the high selectivity of Ce-N-CDs toward Fe3+.

The metal ion sensing capability of Ce-N-CDs was evaluated by examining their response to varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions [46]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the addition of Fe3+ ions (0 to 10 μM) concentration led to a gradual decrease in photoluminescence (PL) intensity due to PL quenching. This quenching behavior can be attributed to the strong coordination between Fe3+ ions and the surface oxygen-containing functional groups on the Ce-N-CDs. Fe3+ ions are known to induce PL quenching through static or dynamic mechanisms [49], either by forming non-emissive complexes with fluorophores (static quenching) or through non-radiative energy transfer (dynamic quenching). The degree of quenching depends on the sensitivity of the sensing system; a highly sensitive system can detect even minor changes in Fe3+ concentration. To investigate the stoichiometry of the interaction between Ce-N-CDs and Fe3+ ions, a Job’s plot (continuous variation method) was measured. As shown in Fig. 4e, PL intensity at 553 nm was analyzed as a function of the molar ratio of [Fe3+] to [Fe3+ + Ce-N-CDs]. The maximum PL quenching occurred at 0.5 mol fraction, representative a 1:1 binding ratio of Fe3+ ion and Ce-N-CDs. The Stern–Volmer plot was used to further examine the quenching mechanism of Ce-N-CDs by Fe3+ ions [49] (Fig. 4f). The linear relationship observed between PL intensity and Fe3+ concentration (0–10 μM) suggests either a static or dynamic quenching process. The high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9963) indicates excellent linearity, reflecting a strong and predictable interaction between Fe3+ ions and Ce-N-CDs [51]. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was calculated using the formula: LOD = K x SD/S, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD was determined to be 34.6 nM (Fig. 4g), demonstrating the high sensitivity of Ce-N-CDs toward Fe3+ detection. Because of their low detection limit, Ce-N-CDs are an excellent choice for detecting Fe3+ in environmental and biological systems. In particular, the LOD is substantially lower than the USEPAmaximum allowable amount of Fe3+ in drinking water [61], demonstrating the applicability of this reaction-based sensing technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference effect of Fe3+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: The interference study was carried out to determine the selectivity of Ce-N-CDs for Fe3+ detection in the presence of different anions and biomolecules by analyzing their PLresponses. The results indicated that the majority of examined anions and biomolecules had minimal impact on the PL intensity of Ce-N-CDs, demonstrating their outstanding selectivity for Fe3+ ions. However, the addition of pyrophosphate (PPi) notably enhanced the PL intensity (Fig. 5 a), suggesting a potential competitive interaction [46,49]. In this scenario, PPi likely binds to Fe3+ ions, thereby preventing their quenching interaction with Ce-N-CDs. This observation not only reinforces the high selectivity of Ce-N-CDs for Fe3+ but also indicates their potential utility in detecting PPi, making them promising candidates for biological and environmental applications [50,51,62]. Furthermore, the PL histogram data presented in Fig. 5b clearly illustrates the ability of the Ce-N-CDs-based sensor to effectively distinguish PPi from other anions and biomolecules, highlighting its dual-sensing capability.

The sensitivity of Ce-N-CDs toward PPi detection was evaluated by monitoring the PL spectra of Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ aqueous solutions upon the incremental addition of PPi (0–50 μM) in PBS, pH ∼ 7.4 solution. The results revealed a concentration-dependent recovery of PL intensity, indicating that PPi effectively counteracts Fe3+-induced PL quenching of Ce-N-CDs. This behavior reflects a strong binding affinity between PPi and Fe3+, which competes with the Fe3+–Ce-N-CDs interaction [46,49]. The formation of a Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+/PPi complex leads to PL restoration (Fig. 5c), demonstrating the high sensitivity of Ce-N-CDs for PPi detection. To determine the binding stoichiometry, a Job’s plot was constructed by plotting PL intensity against the mole fraction of PPi. The PL intensity at 0.5 mol fraction, signifying a 1:1 binding ratio between PPi and Fe3+ (Fig. 5d). This confirms that one PPi molecule binds to one Fe3+ ion, effectively disrupting the Fe3+-Ce-N-CDs complex and regenerating the PL signal. The 1:1 stoichiometry highlights the strong and specific coordination between Fe3+ and PPi, which is critical for the observed PL recovery. These findings support the potential application of Ce-N-CDs as a selective and sensitive PL sensor for PPi detection in biological and environmental contexts. In addition, Benesi–Hildebrand plot was employed to further assess PL recovery of the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ system with increasing PPi concentrations [50,51] (0–50 μM). The resulting linear plot (Fig. 5e), with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9951), confirms a uniform and predictable interaction between PPi and the Fe3+-quenched Ce-N-CDs complex. This linearity underscores the reliability and reproducibility of the PL response toward PPi, supporting its use in trace-level detection. The calculated LOD was 25.4 nM (Fig. 5f), demonstrating the excellent sensitivity of the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ system for PPi recognition. This low detection limit indicates the strong potential of Ce-N-CDs as a powerful and selective PL sensor for detecting trace amounts of PPi in environmental and biological samples.

To evaluate the reversibility and sensing capability of Ce-N-CDs, photoluminescence (PL) spectra and intensities were measured for Ce-N-CDs, Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+, and Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ with PPi in PBS (pH ∼ 7.4). Pristine Ce-N-CDs exhibited strong PL intensity, which was considerably quenched upon the Fe3+addition, indicating the formation of a Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ complex. However, upon subsequent adding of PPi, a gradual recovery in PL intensity was detected, confirming that PPi effectively displaced Fe3+ from the complex and reversed the quenching effect (Fig. 6 a). This “on–off–on” PL switching behavior demonstrates the excellent reversibility and recovery ability of Ce-N-CDs in detecting Fe3+ and PPi. The efficient PL restoration suggests that Ce-N-CDs can serve as a highly selective and recyclable sensing platform for Fe3+ and PPi detection in both biological and environmental contexts [46,49].

To evaluate the stability and reusability of Ce-N-CDs as a PL sensor, reversible PL switching cycles were conducted by consecutively adding Fe3+ and PPi in pH ∼ 7.4 PBS solution. Initially, Ce-N-CDs displayed intense PL, which was quenched by Fe3+ addition due to complex formation. Upon subsequent addition of PPi, the PL was restored, indicating that PPi effectively chelated Fe3+ and disrupted the Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ complex. This “on–off–on” switching cycle was repeated multiple times (Fig. 6b), exhibiting consistent and reversible PL changes [50,51], thereby confirming the robust reversibility and stability of Ce-N-CDs as a dual sensor. The reproducible PL response over multiple cycles highlights Ce-N-CDs as a durable, reusable, and highly selective PL sensor for the detection of Fe3+ and PPi in environmental and biological samples.

The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of Ce-N-CDs were evaluated using a WST assay with HCT-116 cells after 24 h of incubation. The results revealed no significant cytotoxicity, with cell viability remaining above 90 % even at 75 μg/mL concentration [58](Fig. 7 a). This extraordinary cell viability demonstrates the excellent biocompatibility of Ce-N-CDs, assembly them favorable candidates for biological applications such as bioimaging and biosensing [63]. The observed minimal toxicity can be attributed to their carbon-based composition and surface modifications, which minimize harmful cellular interactions. To explore the intracellular sensing capability of Ce-N-CDs, bright-field and fluorescence microscopy were conducted under various treatment conditions (Fig. 7b). Cells treated with Ce-N-CDs exhibited bright intracellular yellow emission, confirming effective uptake and cytoplasmic localization [49]. In contrast, cells treated with Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ showed significantly quenched fluorescence, and the fluorescence was restored upon addition of PPi [58]. Under identical imaging conditions, no significant background fluorescence was observed in the control group, supporting the selective fluorescence of the Ce-N-CDs probe. This “on–off–on” fluorescence switching behavior confirms the reversible sensing capability of Ce-N-CDs for Fe3+ and PPi within live cells. The clear recovery of intracellular fluorescence demonstrates that Ce-N-CDs can function as sensitive and selective intracellular probes, applicable for real-time monitoring, bioimaging, and diagnostic analysis of Fe3+ homeostasis and phosphate metabolism [64].

In summary, cerium- and nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Ce-N-CDs) were successfully synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, using ribavirin as the nitrogen source and cerium nitrate as the metal precursor. The resulting Ce-N-CDs were comprehensively characterized to confirm their chemical composition and structural features, revealing the presence of functional groups associated with both cerium and nitrogen by UV–Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. The resulting Ce-N-CDs exhibited a quantum yield of 17.2 %, demonstrating strong fluorescence efficiency suitable for sensing and imaging applications. TEM images of high resolution showed that Ce-N-CDs had an average diameter of approximately 3.62 ± 0.2 nm, a range of 2.5 – 5.5 nm, and an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm. Furthermore, The chemosensor Ce-N-CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions and PPi, which exhibited turn-off/on photoluminescence and a considerable red shift with yellow emission. In addition, lower detection limit and good linear relation for Fe3+ and PPi were obtained as 34.6 nM and 25.4 nM, respectively with broad range of Fe3+ is 0–10 M and PPi is 0–50 M. Thereafter, the intensified PL of Ce-N-CDs/Fe3+ complex system was capable of sensing PPi in the water solution via displacement strategy with excellent reversibility. Additionally, the photoluminescent Ce-N-CDs was successfully used for Fe3+ and PPi detection in live cells. Therefore, this novel proposed method improves sensitivity, selectivity, and biocompatibility, making Ce-N-CDs a suitable platform for potential biological and environmental monitoring.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sonaimuthu Mohandoss He received a doctoral degree from Alagappa University and has 10 years of research experience in Materials Chemistry, Supramolecular Chemistry, Organic Synthesis for Chemical Sensors, Computational Chemistry & Bioimaging Applications. He is the recipient of several awards including Basic Scientific Research-Junior Research Fellow (BSR-JRF) and extended Senior Research Fellow (BSR-SRF) at University Grant Commission (UGC), India (2011–2015). He received the award (Ministry of Science and Technology) MOST Fellowship for Post-Doctoral Fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Sun Yat-Sen University, Taiwan (2017–2018). Moreover, he worked as a Post-Doctoral Researcher in School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea (2018–2020). Currently, he is working as a Research Professor at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Republic of Korea. His research interests are focused on organic materials and small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for Chemosensor, environmental, and live cell imaging.

Alagarsamy Priyadharshini She received her B.Sc. (2021) and M.Sc. (2023) degree in Chemistry at Madurai Kamaraj University, India. Now she is doing doctoral research under the supervision of Prof. Dr. A. A. Napoleon at the Department of Chemistry, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore, India. Her research focuses on the design and synthesis of small molecules-derived carbon nanomaterials for sensor applications, including the selective detection of metal ions, real sample analysis, and live cell imaging.

Kuppu Sakthi Velu He completed his Ph.D. in 2021 under the guidance of Dr. T. Stalin at the School of Chemical Sciences, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi-03, Tamil Nadu, and India. He then worked as a postdoctoral fellow at SSN College of Engineering, Anna University, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India (2021-2022), and subsequently at Yeungnam University, South Korea (2022-2023). Currently, he serves as an International Research Professor (Assistant Professor) in the School of Chemical Engineering at Yeungnam University, South Korea. His research focuses on MXene-based heterostructured materials for applications in energy conversion, energy storage, and biomedical fields.

Ayyakannu Arumugam Napoleon Dr. A.A. Napoleon is an avid researcher and Professor at VIT University, Vellore. He has completed his Ph.D. in 2015 under the guidance of Dr. F. Nawaz Khan, from Department of Chemistry, VIT University, Vellore on the topic of “Studies on synthesis, biological evaluations of few nitrogen and oxygen heterocycles (pyrazolines, isochromen-1- ones, xanthenediones). He has completed his master degree M.Pharm from Birla Institute of Technology in 1997 and Bachelor degree B.Pharm from The Tamilnadu Dr MGR Medical University, Chennai, India in 1994. He has published over 60 Research papers with good impact factor journal publications. His current research work is primarily focuses on the development of probe as Chemosensor for metal ion sensing applications, environmental, and live cell imaging and developing Carbon Nanomaterials-Carbon dots, Also designing novel heterocyclic compounds and exploring for suitable pharmacological applications from synthetic medicinal chemistry and from natural products.

Prasanta Roy Dr. Prasanta Roy obtained his Ph.D. under the supervision of Prof. Abu T. Khan and Prof. B. K. Patel from Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Guwahati, India in 2016. He worked as postdoctoral fellow at Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China (2016–2018), Yunnan University, China (2019–2021), Indian Institute of Technology, Kanpur, India (2021–2022) and Yeungnam University, South Korea (2022–2023). Presently, he is Assistant Professor at School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea. His research interests include C−H activation, Asymmetric Organic Synthesis, Click Chemistry, Multi-component Reaction and Medicinal Chemistry.

Naushad Ahmad Dr. Ahmad did his Ph.D from the prestigious Central University, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi India in 2009 on the topic of “Synthesis of bridged bimetallic complexes: Characterization and physico-chemical studies”. He has completed his master and Bachelor degree from the Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, U.P. India. His current Research interest is Chemical sensors, Biosensors, Supercapacitors, H2 production, petroleum refining, Battery, Perovskites, Nanomaterials, Ferrites, Metal oxides, Graphene and heterogeneous systems. He has published more than 150 research articles in reputed International SCI Journals. Current Research interest: Synthesis of nanostructured materials (metal, metal oxides, carbon nanotubes, and graphene) and their applications as a sensor, supercapacitor, and in conversion reactions (DRM, SRM and alcohols).

Subramanian Palanisamy He is a working as a Research Professor at the East Coast Life Sciences Institute, Gangneung-Wonju National University, Republic of Korea. He completed his M.Sc and Ph.D. in the Department of Animal Health and Management, Alagappa University, Karaikudi, Tamil Nadu, India. During his doctoral and pre-doctoral research, he worked on polysaccharides, nanomaterials, and their biological activities. His current research interests include the biological activities of natural polysaccharides, the development of polysaccharides conjugates for drug delivery, and the synthesis of green nanomaterials for biological applications.

SangGuan You He is currently a Professor in the Department of Marine Food Science and Technology, College of Life Science, Republic of Korea. He earned his Bachelor’s and Master’s degrees from Gangneung National University and Korea University in 1997 and 1999, respectively. He completed his Doctorate in Food Engineering, University of Manitoba, Canada, and later worked as a Senior Researcher at the University of Manitoba (2005). He is a member of the American Association of Cereal Chemist and serves as an editorial board member of Bioactive Carbohydrates and Dietary Fiber. His current research focuses on the structural analysis of polysaccharides, the preparation of polysaccharide conjugates for drug delivery, and their applications in pharmaceutical activities, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anticancer, and immunostimulatory properties.

Seong-Cheol Kim Prof. Seong-Cheol Kim is a faculty member at the School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, South Korea, specializing in Polymer Science and Engineering. He earned his Ph.D. in Polymer Science & Engineering from the University of Massachusetts Lowell (2001–2006) and an M.Eng. in Chemical Engineering from Seoul National University (1998–2000). His research focuses on advanced materials, including polymers, nanomaterials, and biomaterials, with applications in energy systems and renewable technologies. At Yeungnam University, he is part of a dynamic team advancing innovation through government-backed initiatives like the Brain Korea 21st Century programs, aimed at fostering emerging technologies in energy systems and green materials​.
